pattern: Because waiting at a room never hurts and every move costs exactly one second, the earliest arrival time at each room is a shortest-path problem where 'distance' along an edge means waiting for the room to open before spending the one-second move, which a priority-queue shortest-path search handles directly.
hints: From a room you're standing in at some time t, moving into a neighboring room costs one second, but you cannot start that move until the neighbor's opening time has passed -- so your arrival time there is whichever is later: your current time, or the room's opening time, plus one.

This is just a shortest-path search where the 'edge weight' from one room to an adjacent one depends on when you arrive at the first room, not on a fixed number -- a priority queue that always expands the currently-earliest-known arrival time handles this correctly.

Keep searching outward from the start room until you've settled the earliest arrival time at the bottom-right room; there is no reason to ever revisit a room once its earliest arrival time is finalized.

edge_inputs.description: small grid where waiting is required before the first move
every room already open, pure travel time
all rooms share the same large opening time
the direct path is blocked by a late-opening room, forcing a detour around it
the start room itself has a nonzero opening time, which never matters since you begin there at time zero
opening times at the upper bound

stress.description: random grids with random opening times
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(2, max(2, min(size, 40)))
    m = rng.randint(2, max(2, min(size, 40)))
    grid = [[rng.randint(0, 1000000000) for _ in range(m)] for _ in range(n)]
    grid[0][0] = 0
    return [grid]

complexity.time: O(n*m*log(n*m)) with a priority queue (the authored JS reference uses a simpler O((n*m)^2) linear scan)
